data.title: Budget airlines ask Trump for $2.5 million bailout: bad idea!
data.description:
We’ve seen this movie before, but this time, it’s happening mid-conflict.  

Budget airlines are now asking the Trump administration for $2.5 billion in relief, and at the center of it all is Spirit Airlines — a struggling carrier that the president has openly floated the idea of just buying outright.  

On the one hand, there is a real crisis happening. The war involving the U.S., Israel and Iran has sent jet fuel prices soaring, in some cases doubling, and that is crushing airlines, especially the low-cost ones that survive on razor-thin margins. These are the carriers that make travel accessible for everyday Americans.  

But on the other hand, we’re talking about taxpayer money stepping in to rescue private companies. Once again.  

This is probably one of the only times Elizabeth Warren and Ted Cruz will ever agree. They both think this is a “terrible idea” — that’s a direct quote from Cruz. 

The president wants to buy Spirit, framing it as a way to save jobs and potentially turn a profit when selling it later. You’ve got lawmakers like Warren questioning whether this is a responsible use of taxpayer dollars, while conservatives like Cruz are warning this starts to look like government-run business. It’s something Republicans I thought, have traditionally opposed.  

And here’s the nuance that matters: this isn’t 2020.  

During COVID, airlines got $54 billion in aid because the entire travel system was collapsing overnight. That was about preventing economic freefall.  

This? This is different.  

Yes, the fuel crisis is real. Yes, jobs are at stake. But larger airlines are already finding ways to offset costs — raising fares, adjusting operations — and demand hasn’t collapsed.  

Once the government steps in like this, it doesn’t just stabilize, it reshapes the rules.  

This debate isn’t just about airlines. It’s about how far we’re willing to go when global conflict starts hitting Americans at home, in their wallets, in their jobs, and in the costs of everyday life.  

Lindsey Granger is a NewsNation contributor and co-host of The Hill’s commentary show “Rising.” This column is an edited transcription of her on-air commentary.

data.title: WHCA shooting suspect's motivations emerge: What we know
data.description:
A picture of the alleged gunman at the White House Correspondents’ Association dinner has started to emerge ahead of his initial court appearance Monday.  

The suspect has been identified as Cole Allen, 31, a teacher and game developer who law enforcement says traveled from California to Washington, D.C. to violently disrupt the annual black-tie dinner for journalists and public officials. 

Here’s what we know so far about the suspect’s alleged motivations.  

Suspect’s writings suggest Trump admin targets 

Authorities are still investigating the alleged gunman’s motives for attempting to breach the dinner with a shotgun, handgun and knives in tow. But the suspect’s own writings appear to illuminate his intentions.  

In a manifesto first reported by the New York Post, which an administration official confirmed to The Hill was written by the suspect, Allen refers to himself as a “Friendly Federal Assassin.” 

He allegedly wrote that Trump administration officials were “targets, prioritized from highest-ranking to lowest,” while guests at the dinner and employees were “not targets at all.” 

However, he said he would “still go through most everyone here to get to the targets if it were absolutely necessary,” contending that the decision to attend “a speech by a pedophile, rapist and traitor” makes attendees complicit.  

A U.S. Secret Service agent was shot amid the gunman’s sprint toward the ballroom, but the bullet struck his protective vest. Law enforcement says the suspect is believed to have acted alone.   

The suspect was a guest at the hotel, having checked in before the day of the dinner, according to police. In his apparent writings, Allen criticized lax security for the event. 

“Like, this level of incompetence is insane,” the suspect allegedly wrote, “and I very sincerely hope it’s corrected by the time this country gets actually competent leadership again.” 

The New York Post also reported that an account on the social media platform Bluesky, apparently belonging to Allen and using the handle “coldforce,” which was also mentioned in the manifesto, made repeated critical posts against Trump and his administration. The account has since been suspended.  

Family members cite political grievances  

Allen’s family members have spoken to law enforcement about the incident and his alleged manifesto.  

A White House official told The Hill that Allen’s sister informed police and Secret Service that her brother had a tendency to “make radical statements,” frequently referencing a plan to do “something” to address issues with today’s world.  

She also confirmed that her brother purchased two handguns and a shotgun, which were stored at their parents’ home without their knowledge.  

Allen’s brother notified New London, Conn. police of the alleged manifesto just minutes before the incident at the Washington Hilton, where the dinner was being held, the White House official said.  

A LinkedIn account that appears to belong to Allen describes him as a “mechanical engineer and computer scientist by degree, independent game developer by experience, teacher by birth.” He is from Torrance, Calif. 

Allen holds a master’s degree in computer science from California State University-Dominguez Hills, and an undergraduate degree in mechanical engineering from the California Institute of Technology, the LinkedIn page shows. He works as a teacher.  

Investigation continues with court appearance expected 

Jeanine Pirro, U.S. attorney for the District of Columbia, said at a press conference late Saturday that the suspect faces two charges so far: using a firearm during a crime of violence, and assault on a federal officer using a dangerous weapon.  

Surveillance video posted by Trump appeared to capture the alleged gunman’s dash toward the ballroom, where the dinner’s attendees dropped to the floor and senior officials were ushered out of the room after shots rang out. 

Pirro signaled that additional charges are likely, and acting Attorney General Todd Blanche suggested that the suspect could “absolutely” be charged with attempting to assassinate Trump. 

Blanche said law enforcement has executed search warrants on Allen’s devices and are continuing to gather evidence, adding that the FBI was working through the night.  

The suspect is expected to make his initial appearance in federal court this afternoon. Top officials, including Blanche, Pirro and FBI Director Kash Patel, will give a press conference afterwards to formally announce federal charges.

data.title: Congress must modernize asylum policy before the justices upend it
data.description:
An immigration case currently in front of the Supreme Court may unintentionally nudge asylum policy toward more illegal crossings.

In one of several important immigration-related cases, the Supreme Court heard oral arguments in March in Mullins v. Al Otro Lado, about whether the U.S. government can prevent people seeking asylum from crossing into the U.S. at ports of entry. Traditionally, asylum seekers have been allowed into the country to file their petitions from U.S. soil.

The decision will likely affect whether more people will try to cross into the U.S. between ports of entry. But fortunately, the U.S. doesn’t need the Supreme Court to build a modern immigration approach that considers both security and human dignity. The ultimate power to decide how we should manage asylum at the border resides in Congress, not with the various and at times contradictory decisions by courts or changes in the Oval Office.

Known as a “metering” case, Mullins v. Al Otro Lado addresses a policy initiated by the Obama administration in 2016 and continued in the first Trump administration of limiting the number of people who can seek asylum each day at ports of entry. Although it is not in effect now, the current Trump administration wants the option to reinstate the policy if large numbers of asylum-seekers should again arrive at the border.

While we wait for the Supreme Court ruling later this year, we can get a sense of the justices’ thinking from their questions and comments during oral arguments. They focused on whether the law’s requirement that the migrant must “arrive” in the U.S. to request asylum means that they must be physically on U.S. soil.

If the Supreme Court says they must, and the government won’t let them in, that would likely create two undesirable outcomes: Thousands of migrants will be forced to wait in makeshift refugee camps in Mexico, and many will decide to pay human smugglers to help them cross the border between ports of entry, ensuring they are on U.S. soil to make their asylum request.

This would again create chaos and instability, but Congress could avoid all that with a stronger approach to managing regional migration.

Most migrants at the border are subject to expedited removal — a faster deportation process for recent arrivals designed to deter crossings. Congress created expedited removal in 1996, with an exception to allow asylum applications. Initially only used at ports of entry, expedited removal and the asylum exception were expanded in 2005 to apply between ports of entry, too.

This change didn’t fundamentally alter border operations for several years. When the demographics of border arrivals changed to families and children in 2013, that exception rapidly overwhelmed the process. By 2024, hundreds of thousands of migrants per month were requesting asylum. While the border is quiet now, future policy changes or regional crises could change that, forcing the U.S. to once again manage modern migration challenges with outdated policy and process.

It’s difficult to maintain facilities or surge capacity throughout the immigration system to address large increases in migration, a major reason that current border asylum rules are not sustainable. Three different Cabinet departments manage parts of the asylum process for different populations, and each has a separate appropriations committee and annual bill in Congress.

In an era of persistent deficits, Congress doesn’t generally spend money on unneeded facilities or personnel. And coordinating these resources across departments doesn’t happen.

The United States is still under international treaty obligation to preserve protection for genuine refugees, but it can create orderly processes, particularly for border arrivals. As a recent article for the George W. Bush Institute argued, immigration policy is increasingly entwined with foreign policy. The executive branch, primarily through the Department of State and Department of Homeland Security, should engage with neighboring countries to manage migrants who enter or pass through their territories with the intent to seek asylum. These agencies can work with our regional allies to:

  • Revise refugee processes in contiguous and other countries in the hemisphere;
  • Develop screening and application processes before migrants reach the border;
  • Revise the process for encountering asylum-seekers who arrive at the border; and
  • Update the process for deciding asylum applications for those who are granted access to it at the border.

While these recommendations would be a good start, legislation regarding asylum at the border must also be updated — a responsibility that is wholly on Congress.

While no new legislation has been enacted, several bills introduced recently have laid the foundation for such changes: creating limits on asylum availability to those who cross illegally, investing in screening processes without requiring people to come to the border, increasing refugee processing in the hemisphere for those who qualify, and creating more speedy asylum decisions for those who apply at the land borders.

How we address these issues will determine whether the U.S. can maintain its status as a beacon of safety for those suffering from persecution while maintaining a secure border. We believe it can and should be done.

Theresa Brown is an immigration fellow at the George W. Bush Institute, where Laura Collins is the director of immigration policy.

data.title: Live updates: Leavitt calls DHS shutdown 'shameful' in wake of WHCA dinner shooting
data.description:
White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt opened Monday’s press briefing by calling for peaceful debate and protest, an end to “the hateful and constant and violent rhetoric against President Trump” and for Congress to fund the Department of Homeland Security, which has been shut down for two and a half months. She described the shutdown as “shameful.”

The suspected gunman involved in Saturday’s shooting at the White House Correspondents’ Association (WHCA) dinner is slated to appear in court Monday. Early indications are that likely he was targeting President Trump and other top officials.

On Capitol Hill, meanwhile, Senate Republicans say they’re losing confidence in Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth’s leadership, with some saying they’d like to see him “move on.”

Both the Senate and House are in session — here’s what to expect this week.

The Supreme Court will hear arguments Monday in a case with potentially major implications for how law enforcement investigates crimes in the digital age. Also on the docket are arguments in a case that could curtail antipesticide lawsuits — an issue that pits the Make America Healthy Again movement against the Trump administration.

King Charles III and Queen Camilla will also be making their way to the U.S. on Monday for a six-day visit. They’ll try to smooth over relations with the U.K., but the controversies surrounding convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein, however, threaten to cast a shadow over the visit.

Trump on Monday will attend a series of meetings before greeting the British royals alongside first lady Melania Trump at the White House.

Follow live below.

data.title: Obama calls on Americans to reject violence after WHCA dinner shooting
data.description:
Former President Obama on Sunday urged Americans to reject violence following a shooting at the White House Correspondents Dinner over the weekend. 

“Although we don’t yet have the details about the motives behind last night’s shooting at the White House Correspondents Dinner, it’s incumbent upon all us to reject the idea that violence has any place in our democracy,” Obama wrote in a statement on X.

“It’s also a sobering reminder of the courage and sacrifice that U.S. Secret Service Agents show every day. I’m grateful to them – and thankful that the agent who was shot is going to be okay,” he added.

White House officials and FBI agents will be reviewing security protocols for the president following a targeted attack on Saturday at the annual dinner. 

Cole Allen, 31, was taken into police custody and is being investigated as the alleged shooter. 

Authorities say Allen had a manifesto that touted anti-Christian and anti-Trump rhetoric. 

The attack follows a recent increase in political violence, including the political assassination of Turning Point USA founder Charlie Kirk, Minnesota state Rep. Melissa Hortmann and two previous assassination attempts on President Trump.

In the wake of an uptick in violence, Rep. Ro Khanna (D-Calif.) has proposed the creation of a bipartisan national commission for political violence.

“We should look at social media. We should look at mental health issues. We should look at language. But we need to do something to bring the temperature down,” he said during a Sunday appearance on NBC’s “Meet the Press.”

Sen. Thom Tillis (R-N.C.) shared similar observations. 

“In many cases, we’re seeing people who are committing these horrible acts have behavioral health and other challenges, stability issues in their lives,” Tillis said during an appearance on “Meet the Press.”,

He later added that what “American people need to do is take a breath, confirm their facts, talk to their elected officials, think a bit before they judge people.”

The North Carolina senator also urged elected officials to be cautious of their public remarks.

“Our words matter. The weight of our words matter, and we need to be very measured in the way that we use them,” he said in the interview with Kristen Welker.

data.title: Cole Tomas Allen manifesto revealed: WHCD suspect motivated by Epstein files?
data.description:
Another would-be assassin has failed, thank God. Cole Tomas Allen, a 31-year-old engineer and teacher from California, attempted to target President Trump and administration officials at the White House correspondents’ dinner on Saturday night. He brought guns and knives to the dinner’s venue, the Washington Hilton Hotel, where he had checked in as a guest. Thankfully the Secret Service apprehended Allen as he attempted to enter the checkpoint, and he did not get close to the president. 

The failed assassin released a manifesto minutes before carrying out his plan. That manifesto was obtained by The New York Post and other outlets. In it, Allen explains that his plan was to kill as many administration officials as possible.  

As for his motivation, he wrote this: “I am a citizen of the United States of America. What my representatives do reflects on me. And I am no longer willing to permit a pedophile, rapist, and traitor to coat my hands with his crimes.” 

While it’s not specifically stated, I presume that’s a reference to Trump, in which case, we should be very clear about what seems to have motivated Allen.  

First and foremost, he refers to Trump as a pedophile. That can only logically be a reference to one thing: the abjectly false smear that Trump was complicit in the crimes of Jeffrey Epstein. 

Now let me state this plainly: When a crazy person commits violence, or tries to commit violence, I do not assign blame to anyone but him. That makes me a bit unusual, I’ll be honest. Left and right, Republican and Democrat, mainstream media and independent, commentators just love to say that the extreme rhetoric of such and such person or group is the real reason for the violence.  

You see this when plausibly right-wing individuals perpetrate mass violence: We’re told it’s the fault of Trump, or Fox News, for inciting hatred. Or, if the violent individual is on the other side, then were told it’s the Dems fault. This happens all the time. 

But I say, Cole Tomas Allen is responsible for himself, and no one else — no one who criticizes Trump or the administration should be blamed. 

All that said, we need to be honest with ourselves. According to his own manifesto, this would-be assassin thought he had to take action to kill a pedophile president. The idea that Trump is a pedophile is a false, malicious smear, one foisted on the public by conspiracy theorists and unfortunately, for cynical reasons, the leaders of the Democratic Party. They have bought into an obscene lie, that Trump was involved with Epstein’s abuse of underage girls — an accusation for which there is zero proof. 

Now this desperate deception is poisoning the minds of Americans like Cole Tomas Allen, who decided to take matters into his own hands. Again, that’s his crime, and he should be punished for it — Allen and no one else. But we don’t need our political leaders to push wholly unfounded lies like the Epstein smear. It’s wrong and it’s bad and it’s having some very terrifying results. 

CNN’s Dana Bash did not put this exact question to Democrat Jamie Raskin, but she landed on some version of it. Here he was responding. Raskin has been front and center promoting the idea that the administration is covering up aspects of the Epstein files because they want to shield Trump, or the Trump family, or Trump’s associates.  

He accused the administration of doing just that because they hadn’t yet moved swiftly enough to release the files. You might remember that was right around the time that Democrats falsely accused several random men of being criminally indicated in the files, even though they were part of a police lineup that had nothing to do with Epstein. So maybe there was a reason to move just a little bit cautiously — a reason other than Trump being a pedophile. 

Look, you can oppose Trump, you can work against his policies, but if you are still advancing this specific line of attack against the president — well, what can I say? I hope you’re very happy with yourself. 

Robby Soave is co-host of The Hill’s commentary show “Rising” and a senior editor for Reason Magazine. This column is an edited transcription of his daily commentary.

data.title: How HUD is restoring fairness to housing
data.description:
April is Fair Housing Month, when the Department of Housing and Urban Development (HUD) commemorates the signing of the Fair Housing Act and remembers our role in ensuring equality under the law. This Fair Housing Month offers a perfect opportunity to showcase HUD’s commitment to enforcing the Fair Housing Act and restoring the fundamental constitutional principle that all Americans are equal in the eyes of the law. 

Fair housing protections are simple: they prohibit intentional discrimination in housing. But, like so many other aspects of American law, the Biden administration twisted the plain meaning of the Fair Housing Act from protecting individual rights to promoting an equity-based framework of social engineering. Instead of enforcing equal rights, the Biden administration gave some groups extra rights and unfair advantages, and Americans suffered as a result. 

This distortion of fair housing came from years of leftist ideology that spread from academia through social institutions. In a profound betrayal of constitutional principles, HUD weaponized the Fair Housing Act to focus primarily on racial equity objectives then aggressively penalized Americans who did not follow suit.

The good news is that under President Trump, our team at HUD is finally restoring equality and sanity to fair housing enforcement.

In January, we proposed a rule which eliminates disparate impact liability in HUD regulations. For those who aren’t familiar, disparate impact is a legal theory that infers discrimination based on outcomes alone, rather than discriminatory actions — and under Biden, this was HUD’s primary tool for manufacturing fair housing claims.

But when President Trump returned to office, HUD began to dismiss the cases where disparate impact was stretched beyond any reasonable measure.

In one of the most egregious cases, Biden’s HUD colluded with the City of Chicago to stop a recycling facility from coming to the Windy City’s South Side under this disparate impact theory. This action cost hundreds of new jobs because leftist activists alleged the new facility would somehow hurt the “environmental justice” rights of minorities in the city.

But that wasn’t all. Biden’s HUD also used disparate impact theory to force banks to give preferential loans to potential homebuyers on the basis of race, a move that played politics with taxpayer money and denied Americans of certain races and ethnic groups their right to equal treatment under the law.

In one of the most blatantly political cases, we ended a Biden-era civil rights investigation of the Texas General Land Office (GLO), whose “offense” (according to the Biden administration) was fairly and impartially distributing disaster mitigation funds on a race-neutral basis. As GLO Commissioner Dawn Buckingham stated, the claims against the Texas GLO were “false and politically motivated,” and our work has “set the record straight once and for all.” It demonstrated how far the past administration was willing to go to discredit neutral and well-founded criteria in order to allege racism, even without any allegation of intentional discrimination.

At HUD, we are prioritizing actual, provable violations of the Fair Housing Act — not convoluted theories of imaginary trespasses that offend the sensibilities of lawyers and activists.

I have always stood for the principle that exclusionary communities have no place in America, including in my home state of Texas. I am proud to share that HUD acted to enforce that principle by opening an investigation into the East Plano Islamic Center and Community Capital Partners, LP for planning a development that allegedly markets itself as a community for Muslims only, which plainly violates the Fair Housing Act’s prohibition of discrimination on the basis of religion.

Nor are municipalities exempt from fair application of federal law. Unfortunately, they have been some of the worst offenders. For example, HUD has likewise opened investigations of Minneapolis and Boston for implementing housing plans that appear to exclude certain people and favor others based entirely on race.

We also recently launched our first-ever statewide investigation into Washington state’s Covenant Homeownership Program, which privileges “people of color and other historically marginalized communities.” The program went so far as to list favored groups by national ancestry, and applicants who are deemed to not have the “right” ancestry apparently need not apply, all in plain violation of the Fair Housing Act.

Our record of fair housing enforcement shows that we are correcting course at HUD. The plain language of the Fair Housing Act must be our guide, not woke ideology. As we approach our nation’s 250th anniversary, we remember that our story began with the declaration that all men are created equal. Fair Housing Month reminds us that it is our duty to make those words a reality.

Scott Turner is the secretary of Housing and Urban Development.

data.title: Supreme Court grapples with use of 'geofence warrants' by law enforcement
data.description:
The Supreme Court dove into a digital age issue on Monday as it grappled with how to apply constitutional protections against unreasonable searches to cellphone location data. 

Across two hours of arguments, the justices questioned whether the government violated a man’s Fourth Amendment rights when it used a geofence warrant during a bank robbery investigation to identify him. 

The tool compels tech companies to disclose data from customers’ devices at a specific place and time. Investigators typically employ them when they know the time and location of a crime, but not a suspect’s identity.   

As the justices wrestled with the case’s potentially major implications on law enforcement techniques and privacy rights, some expressed concerns about the defendant’s contentions.  

“If you don’t want the government to have your location history, you just flip that off,” Chief Justice John Roberts pressed the defendant’s attorney. “You don’t have to have that feature on your phone. So what’s the issue?” 

Justice Samuel Alito said the defendant had even turned on the service. 

“Had he read his contract with Google, he could see that Google retained the right to turn this information over to law enforcement, if it thought that that was appropriate,” Alito noted. 

The high-stakes battle stems from the criminal prosecution of Okello Chatrie, convicted of robbing a credit union in the Richmond, Va., suburbs in 2019. He pleaded guilty and was sentenced to nearly 12 years in prison, but, all along, Chatrie preserved his constitutional defense against the geofence warrant.  

Law enforcement sought the data from Google after seeing their suspect using a cellphone on the security camera footage. After first receiving anonymized data for devices in a 17.5-acre area around the bank, investigators narrowed their list of suspects and ultimately received Chatrie’s identity. 

The Supreme Court has held the Fourth Amendment’s protections kick in when someone has a “reasonable expectation of privacy.” However, that expectation is lost when the information is shared with a third party. The government says that’s what happened here. 

“Petitioner here is asking for an unprecedented transformation of the Fourth Amendment into an impregnable fortress around records of his public movements that he affirmatively consented to allow Google to create, maintain and use,” Deputy Solicitor General Eric Feigin told the court. 

Some of the justices spend time probing those broad implications. Beyond location data, Google possesses vast information on its millions of customers that use its other services. 

“If we were to rule that there was a voluntary exposure here to Google that allows the government unfettered access to it, that ruling would pertain equally to email,” Justice Neil Gorsuch cautioned. 

Justice Sonia Sotomayor noted Google also offers photo, document and calendar-storing services. 

“So that means the government, police officer, randomly, who decides they don’t like that person, ‘let me just go look at their life to see if I can find a crime,’ that would be okay?” Sotomayor said. 

All sides acknowledge it may raise issues under a federal statute that governs internet communications, which is not before the court. But otherwise, Chatrie’s lawyer agreed that would be the implication. 

“If you’re going to accept that argument, then that is really the end of the Fourth Amendment for any private document or storing with Google,” said Adam Unikowsky, who is a partner at Jenner & Block. 

Geofence warrant usage has grown rapidly in recent years. It constitutes more than a quarter of all U.S. law enforcement demands and played a prominent role in the prosecutions against the Jan. 6, 2021, Capitol rioters. 

Some 31 states, plus the District of Columbia, are backing the Trump administration’s defense of the tool. 

“In practice, geofence warrants have become an important investigative tool for law enforcement, particularly in cases where traditional methods yield few leads or where perpetrators leave little physical evidence,” they wrote in a friend-of-the-court brief. 

As justices across the bench probed the potentially wide-reaching consequences of the case, it left some wondering if they should pursue a narrow path.

“Emails are not before us. Calendars are not before us. Photos are not before us,” Alito said. 

No matter how the constitutional issue shakes out, the lower court ruled Chatrie’s location data could still be admitted as evidence in court because police reasonably believed they were acting lawfully, a principle known as the good-faith exception. 

And, Google in 2023 shifted its data storage policies in a manner that ended its ability to supply location information responsive to geofence warrants.   

Alito suggested that the court’s ruling wouldn’t amount to anything more than a law review article. 

“I’m struggling to understand why we are hearing this case, other than the fact that at least four of us voted to take it,” he said.

Updated at 1:06 p.m. EDT

data.title: Agent shot during Reagan assassination attempt: Secret Service did 'hell of a good job' protecting Trump
data.description:
A Secret Service agent shot during former President Reagan’s 1981 assassination attempt on said on Monday that law enforcement did a “hell of a good job” protecting the president during the White House Correspondents’ Association dinner shooting on Saturday.

Reagan was targeted after leaving a speaking event at the Washington Hilton in 1981. The former president was ultimately struck by gunfire that ricocheted off the presidential limousine, piercing his underarm, breaking a rib, puncturing a lung, and causing serious internal bleeding.

Former agent Tim McCarthy, White House press secretary James Brady, and D.C. police officer Thomas Delahanty were all wounded. Brady suffered permanent brain damages from his injuries.

“From what I can tell right now talking to my colleagues, it was handled pretty well. You never want something like this to happen. Security was tested, security responded, and at this point it did pretty well,” McCarthy said during an appearance on NewsNation’s “NewsNation Live.”

“Tackled him before he even got to the stairs to even get close to coming down by the president. So, so far, based on what I know, I’m pretty satisfied with what the Secret Service did on this occasion,” he added. 

McCarthy’s comments echo White House approval for the Secret Service’s swift evacuation of President Trump and Vice President Vance after shots were fired at the Washington Hilton on Saturday evening. 

White House chief of staff Susie Wiles is planning to hold a meeting with the White House operations team, the Secret Service and Department of Homeland Security leadership to discuss protocol and practice for large events involving Trump.

“The response by the agents that took place appeared to be very good, but they’ll take a look at the advance itself. Is there anything further that could’ve been done? And I’ve heard talk, you know, that you should’ve shut the hotel down,” McCarthy said.

“Well, try finding a hotel with a ballroom if you’re gonna shut down the hotel, because you’re not gonna find one. No one’s gonna want to do that,” he added.

The president suggested using the White House ballroom to host future dinners, but the space is not expected to be completed until 2028. 

McCarthy lauded the fact that the Secret Service had more than three layers of security present for Saturday’s dinner and commended the agency for not allowing the threat to reach the same floor as the ballroom, where Trump was seated. 

“I think we need to ratchet down the rhetoric just a little bit and give the Secret Service at the moment quite a bit of credit for doing a hell of a good job, along with their partners,” he added.

The suspected gunman, Cole Allen, was apprehended and remains in police custody. He’s expected to be arraigned on Monday afternoon for using a firearm during a crime of violence and assault on a federal officer using a dangerous weapon.

Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche said Allen has not been cooperating with law enforcement amid the investigation.

data.title: Watch live: Trump welcomes King Charles III to White House
data.description:
President Trump and first lady Melania Trump will welcome Britain’s King Charles III and Queen Camilla to the White House on Monday.

Buckingham Palace said over the weekend that the trip would proceed after Saturday’s shooting at the White House Correspondents’ Association dinner.

“Following discussions on both sides of the Atlantic through the day, and acting on advice of Government, we can confirm the State Visit by Their Majesties will proceed as planned,” a spokesperson for the palace said Sunday.

The royal tour, which marks Charles’s first trip to the U.S. as monarch, comes Trump has lambasted the country’s Prime Minister Keir Starmer for not being supportive of the U.S. war in Iran and its reluctance to help Washington control the Strait of Hormuz. 

Watch the arrival, scheduled for 4:15 p.m. EDT, in the video player above.
